title: An efficient implementation for solving the all pairs minimax path problem in an undirected dense graph

reviews.summary: The paper proposes and implements an O(n^2) algorithm for computing the all-pairs minimax (a.k.a. widest/bottleneck) path distance matrix on undirected dense graphs by leveraging the classical fact that the path between two nodes in a minimum spanning tree (MST) minimizes the maximum edge along any path in the original graph. The method constructs an MST, processes its edges in descending order, removes each edge to form two components, and fills all cross-component pairwise distances with the edge’s weight, arguing each pair is written exactly once. Experiments in Python (with some C++ baselines for O(n^3) methods) report substantial runtime improvements vs. Floyd–Warshall and a recursive O(n^3) baseline, scaling to 10k nodes.
strengths: The paper gives a simple, implementable procedure that exploits the MST minimax property to fill the full pairwise minimax matrix. The divide-by-largest-edge-and-copy idea is intuitive and can be parallelized.
The emphasis on turning theoretical O(n^2) statements into a concrete implementation is practically motivated.
The study reports wall-clock timings over datasets up to 10k nodes and compares against O(n^3) variants, showing large speedups.
Using both Python and C++ implementations for O(n^3) variants partially contextualizes language effects.
The high-level algorithmic idea is easy to follow, and the Python snippet conveys the intended implementation structure.
The connection to the widest path (maximum capacity) variant and how to adapt the code is clearly stated.
Efficient computation of minimax/LLPD distances is useful to ML tasks (e.g., path-based clustering and robust similarity), and having a practically fast implementation is valuable.
The paper could, in principle, help bridge adoption barriers for path-based methods that rely on this distance.
weaknesses: The core idea is long known: minimax distances equal the maximum MST edge along the MST path; single-linkage dendrogram (SLINK) yields the same ultrametric. The claimed novelty (“first code implementation”) is incorrect; O(n^2) single-linkage implementations and routines that return cophenetic distances (which equal minimax distances under single linkage) exist (e.g., fastcluster, SciPy’s hierarchy + cophenet).

The correctness and O(n^2) complexity arguments are informal. The code repeatedly runs DFS after each edge removal; the total DFS cost needs a careful amortized argument. Sorting edges costs O(n log n), and common MST routines on dense graphs may be O(n^2) or O(n^2 log n) depending on implementation; the paper does not precisely specify the MST routine used to guarantee O(n^2).

The claim that using a bottleneck spanning tree may lead to inaccurate APPD while an MST is accurate is asserted without proof or counterexample; the standard property is that any MST preserves pairwise minimax values via paths in the MST.

Missing the most relevant baselines: single-linkage O(n^2) algorithms (SLINK/CLINK), computing the ultrametric/cophenetic matrix directly from a dendrogram, and MST-based union-find procedures that fill the matrix via recursive bisection. These are precisely the techniques the paper positions itself against.
No correctness validation is presented (e.g., comparing Algorithm 4 outputs to a verified O(n^3) method for small graphs and reporting zero-error rates).

The datasets and weight generation are not described in sufficient detail, and there is no breakdown by graph families (e.g., path-like vs. balanced-tree MSTs) that could affect amortized running time.

The paper contains formatting artifacts, duplicated lines, and inconsistent complexity claims (e.g., MST_shortest_path appears as O(n log n) in a table but O(n^3 log n) in text; the provided implementation suggests at least quadratic-to-cubic behavior).
Several strong claims (e.g., “no code implementation exists,” “theoretical algorithms are impractical”) are not substantiated and are contradicted by widely used libraries and literature.

The paper does not engage with standard single-linkage O(n^2) methods (Sibson’s SLINK and later practical implementations), nor with the common practice of extracting pairwise ultrametric (cophenetic) distances from dendrograms, which are equivalent to the minimax distances under single linkage.
Existing literature on LLPD and practical toolchains (e.g., JMLR’20, hierarchical clustering libraries) is not adequately compared against.
reviews.questions:
1. How do you ensure in your implementation that each pair (p, q) is written exactly once? The provided code does not guard against overwriting, and removing a later (smaller) edge along the u–v path could, in principle, revisit the pair; please clarify with a formal argument or code-level invariant.
2. Which exact MST algorithm and data structures do you use to guarantee O(n^2) on dense graphs? NetworkX’s default implementations may not achieve this; can you provide a self-contained implementation using Prim with an adjacency matrix and report timings?
3. Please include correctness validation: for small/medium graphs, compare Algorithm 4 outputs to those from the O(n^3) Floyd–Warshall variant and report exact match rates.
4. Why do you not compare against single-linkage O(n^2) implementations (e.g., SLINK/fastcluster/SciPy) and report the time to compute the full cophenetic (ultrametric) matrix, which equals the minimax APPD? This seems to be the most relevant baseline.
5. Can you quantify the cumulative cost of the repeated DFS calls used to extract components after each edge removal, and bound it formally for worst-case MST shapes (e.g., a path/tree with high diameter)?
6. The paper claims that using a minimum bottleneck spanning tree (MBST) may yield inaccurate APPD values, whereas an MST is correct. Can you provide a formal proof or a counterexample to support this distinction?
7. The table lists conflicting complexities for MST_shortest_path (O(n log n) vs. O(n^3 log n) in text). Please correct and clarify the true complexity of that baseline and its implementation details.
8. Please release the code and data generation details, including how weights are defined for the datasets, so that results can be reproduced and compared.
reviews.assessment: The paper tackles an important and practical task—efficient computation of all-pairs minimax (widest) path distances on dense undirected graphs—and offers a straightforward implementation based on a well-known MST property. As an engineering note, this could be useful. However, the submission substantially overstates novelty (there are established O(n^2) single-linkage implementations that can produce the same matrix), omits the most relevant O(n^2) baselines, and lacks rigorous correctness and complexity analysis. Presentation quality is also below ICML standards, with formatting artifacts, inconsistencies, and unsubstantiated claims about the absence of code in prior work. With substantial revision—adding formal analysis, thorough correctness tests, strong baselines (SLINK/fastcluster/SciPy cophenetic), precise MST implementation details achieving O(n^2), and improved writing—the work could become a valuable practical reference. In its current form, I do not recommend acceptance at ICML.
